title: Hall-Effect Reed Switch Wheel Speed Sensor
design_question: How to measure bicycle wheel speed accurately using a single MCU interrupt pin?
selected_approach: A3144 unipolar Hall-effect sensor zip-tied to fork; 6 mm × 3 mm N52 neodymium disc magnet epoxied to spoke. 1 pulse/revolution at rising edge on GPIO interrupt. Speed = wheel_circumference / period (ms). 10 kΩ pull-up to 5 V; sensor output open-drain.
rationale: Hall sensors have zero contact wear (vs. mechanical reed switches), operate in wet/muddy conditions, and provide clean digital transitions. A3144 is a proven, inexpensive choice rated −40 °C to +85 °C. Interrupt-driven measurement is CPU-efficient and resolves speeds down to walking pace.

alternatives.reason_rejected: Limited to ~10⁷ operations; 1–2 ms bounce requires hardware debounce; not weatherproof without potting.
Exposed phototransistor fails in rain and mud; requires hub modification.

verification_notes: FACT: 'Hall-Effect Speed Sensor for Rotating Machinery' confirms interrupt-based period measurement and A3144 suitability for single-magnet spoke installations.
supporting_fact_titles: Hall-Effect Speed Sensor for Rotating Machinery
